When only removing the function parameter from the outline view it feels strange when having a function like:
```python
def f(a):
    b = a
    return b
```
The outline view would show:
```bash
f
  a # only if also returning the parameter
  b
```
Which in my opinion is strange since `b` isn't a real property of `f`.

I think it is OK for the language server to make some language specific decisions what values to return.

Implementations in multi language editors like Atom or VSCode cannot easily make language agnostic decisions about displaying. So I think it's OK to provide some sensible defaults from the language server side.

If you check `traceback.format_stack(limit=limit)`, it returns a Python list and you can use a view instead of the full list (eg: `traceback.format_stack(limit=limit)[:-2]` if we should discard the last 2 elements).

In that way our `dd-trace-py` code will not be in the traceback list. If you think it's fine, remember to increase the `limit` value by the same amount you're removing from the full list so if you're `[:-2]`, then `limit += 2`. Otherwise you ask for a limit of 20 but only 18 results are returned.

Here the intent of the "original" author (before https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/c04abca6 broke it by importing the newer module) was to import the global `distutils.sysconfig._config_vars` dictionary, not to get the values, but to temporarily set them before calling `distutils.sysconfig.customize_compiler`. This is only for darwin, where building dynamic load libraries requires special compiler flags.

The problem is the `customize_compiler` function uses the `_config_vars` which are defined in the `distutils.sysconfig` module, not the one from the new `sysconfig` module. Therefor, importing `_CONFIG_VARS` from the latter, and modifying them, does not have any effect on `customize_compiler`.

OK.  I dug into this a little more, and here's the deal:

- That sentence in the docstring implies that things are a bit more deterministic than they actually are.  In other words, that's the optimal case (which results in the solar panels pointing directly at the sun), but some amount of rotation with respect to that orientation is allowed.

- One could, in principle, take an input date and fairly easily calculate the optimal PA.  I mean, I haven't done it but from the documentation they gave me (which is now in the docstring) it would be easy.

- I asked if they have code that calculates the allowed range of PA about the optimal one, and apparently it's very complicated and not in a good form for us to use.

- I could imagine two scenarios in practice.  The first is that the survey or the observer says "I want the following RA, Dec, PA" - and let the scheduler work out what dates that combination of RA, Dec, PA is allowed.  (In some cases they won't be allowed at all, in other cases there will be a wide or narrow range or dates in which it is allowed.)  In this scenario we would want people to be able to specify RA, Dec, PA.  

- The other scenario is that the person says "I want to go stare at this RA, Dec on this date, and don't care much about the PA so just do whatever works best."  In that case, we want the code to know how to calculate the optimal PA for a given date.

I honestly can't decide what would be the best thing to do here.  One option for the near term is to let the observer choose RA, Dec, PA, and simply state in the docstring that PA is actually constrained to some narrow range based on the date.  Then provide a helper routine they can use to calculate the optimal PA for a given date and ```world_pos```, or ignore, depending on how much they care about these details.  Later once there is reliable code to calculate the range of allowed PA we could incorporate that, but I have no idea when that will be. What do you think, given this additional info?

In this case, the main reason for doing this is that lambda expressions are not picklable.  So making it a property means it doesn't actually appear in the `__dict__`, so it doesn't need to be pickled.  Doing this with all three was just for consistency to make them all look the same, but it doesn't really matter much for the other two.

The other two benefits, which are inconsequential in this case, but have been a reason for me to use `@property` in other cases are:
1. Efficiency.  The code only creates the thing to be returned when it needs it rather than on every construction.  If the attribute is rarely needed and non-trivial to make, then this can be a good reason to use a property.  I don't think either the array or the lambda are terribly time consuming to make, so it doesn't matter much here, although maybe it's a slight efficiency gain.
2. Read-only.  I don't think we are worried about users writing other values to these attributes, but in other cases, I've used properties to help enforce the immutability of some of our objects.  You can write setters for properties, but the simple thing I did here means that these are all read-only.

Now, perhaps a more important point about this.  I'm pretty sure the "ecliptic" longitude is not the normal ecliptic coordinate system.  I'm pretty sure this table considers the sun to be at lat, long = 0,0.  That's why the numbers shoot up there and get undefined when you get too close to 0,0.  So I think this routine needs some kind of date of observation to calculate what ecliptic longitude the sun is for this observation.  Right now I believe it is implicitly assuming that the observations are taking place on March 21.

I didn't want to rely on pg_ctl timeout because I still want to allow for manual failover. For example, master_start_timeout is configured to a large number, but sysadmin decides that in this case it's more important to fail over instead. Maybe that usecase could be taken care of by just stopping Patroni. Not sure if it would simplify anything because the current code also handles PostgreSQL restart_after_crash=true behavior (else branch in Postgresql.check_for_startup).

Merging check_for_startup, post_recover and is_healthy could result in some simplification though.

As for stop timing out, this is why demote_asap does stop('immediate', checkpoint=False). That can still fail to stop within a timeout, but there is little we can do from within Patroni except hope that the load balancer health check will mark us unhealthy and take care of redirecting connections. I do have a patch coming up that uses kernel watchdog support to ensure that the node goes away in case things take too long. I made a note to check how other call sites behave in case of stop time outs.

That's what I thought too, but the signature checking logic doesn't work that way. You're correct that if `require_signature` is false, then signatures aren't required. But there's some subtleties here.

If an importer config has `{'require_signature': False, 'allowed_keys': []}`, then any package with may be imported.

If an importer has `{'require_signature': False, 'allowed_keys': ['abcd1234']}`, then:

* Packages with no signatures are allowed.
* Packages with signature `abcd1234` are allowed.
* Packages with any other signature are disallowed.

Imagine the life of a Runnable: `start` -> `run` -> `stop`
- `start` is synchorous, and may raise if something **during** startup didn't go as expected. Think on a port binding when the port is already in use, and it should prevent the task from being actually started (as some pre-start condition failed)
- `runtime` exceptions are raised _during_ "normal" operation, inside `run` blocking call, either by itself (in case its a loop) or by any subtask (through `kill` or `get`)
- `stop` should also be synchronous, and it should fail on **cleanup** exceptions (imagine a `commit` on a DB which failed). No need to re-raise run-time exceptions
